summary: Stancil argues that analytics is inherently creative and messy, not an objective, linear path to truth. Metrics are subjective constructs shaped by business context, so early analytical work should embrace exploration before converging on stable definitions. Attempts to prematurely impose structure or swap tools often mistake the natural mess of discovery for failure.
money_quotes: Data isn’t objective, and analysis isn’t structured. It’s just as creative—and just as messy—as the papers I ran away from in college.
There is no correct win rate waiting to be unearthed; one version isn’t true while another is false.

Our job as analysts isn’t to do the math right so that we can figure out which answer is in the back of the book; it’s to determine which version, out of a subjective set of options, helps us best run a business.
Often, the most useful things we find are the things we weren't looking for.

If you force too much structure on a creative exercise, you’ll end up like me when I did the same thing in my English literature class—with a disappointed professor, and the worst grade on your transcript.
key_insight: Analytics work succeeds when teams accept that defining metrics is a creative, messy process and plan to refine it later rather than forcing premature structure.
